author: @williamfraser
text: The planetary gears are the real treasure. Extremely hard to get hold of otherwise. They often come in multiple stages which means you can customise the desired gear ratio. Even the cheap single speed (36:1) models have two stage reduction gears. I removed the first stage, transferred the pinion to a brusless motor and now have a compact drive for an electric kayak.

author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
text: Nice one Rob. I’ve repurposed two cordless drills by ‘converting’ to mains supplied 18v dc in order to 1. Drive a homemade bench mounted disc sander and 2. Driving one of those drill/chisel sharpener bench mounted systems...they work a treat and, obviously, didn’t therefore get thrown out. Over the years I’ve also bought many 18650s (used for led torches) and would dearly love to simply swap these into dead battery packs but I, excuse the term, sh*t myself about trying to solder them etc and so have never tried. I’ve seen the capacitor based hobby-style  ‘welding’ kits but still have not got the balls to try one. Your vid has rejuvenated my thinking again and given me another idea. Thanks mate 🙂🏴󠁧󠁢󠁷󠁬󠁳󠁿👍

replies.author: @scurvofpcp
replies.text:
It costs a bit more than I like to suggest for entry level, but battery spot welder with 'capacitor energy storage' is what I would suggest to the novice, the results are very reproduceable with those and just order in an extra set of Nickel Strip and have a go for a couple weekends self learning.

Lithium Ion batteries don't like the heat, so this is one case where I really do shy away from using an iron on them although you can buy case holders for those cells as well.

Just make sure to get an active balancer if you plan to do anything too exotic where the pack is likely to never be fully charged/discharged ... such as anything solar based.

replies.text: Ah!!! A bench grinder! Now there's an idea.

author: @Gwydion67
text:
Although not being the first one to tell this story, but...
I'm inmidst replacing the poisenous NiCd batteries of my centuries, pardon decades old 9.6V Bosch drill (still without slipping clutch) by repurposed 18650 ex-notebook Li-cells. 3 of those in series gives a max. of 12.6V (fully charged), voltage dropping slightly under load, resulting in the perfect voltage and endless power! 🤩
I'm planning to use two of those sets in parallel, in short a 3s2p configuration, doubling the operating time of the original battery pack easily. 😎
I'm working on it for half a year now (intermittently), since the devil is in details 😈: I'd like to fit the whole bunch in the original compartment, preferably without major damage to both battery  pack *and* compartment. And this prooves to be a challenge! 😜
Additionally I think about best practices for recharging, since the original charger fits the NiCd charging curve, not the LiCoO2 curve. I will take a closer look to the old charger. Ripping apart the pack every time for recharging woudl be a pain..., so we'll see of what can be done about.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: you might want to keep the ferrite beads in. These are there to reduce radiated emission.

replies.author: @Str8Reckless63
replies.text: Interesting, great tip

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @Str8Reckless63 Well, I sometimes test for radiated and conducted emission. When you have a dc motor like this or a series AC motor, anything with a collector the biggest source of emission is this motor.

replies.author: @Str8Reckless63
replies.text: @hanslepoeter5167 your comment may have stopped me from making a mistake. I was tinkering with a rear projection TV, and after taking apart the lamp housing there was a tiny radiation sticker(Kr85 200 nCi). Something clicked in my mind and I stopped. Without your comment, I may have just continued. Harmful or not, I am thankful for your tip. Appreciate your reply as I didn’t realize it was 2yrs ago ✊

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @Str8Reckless63 Your welcome. Funny though, the radiation sticker you refer to is about radioactive emission. I was talking about electromagnetic waves, eg, radio waves emission for which ferrite beads are effective.. Kr85 is a radioactive isotope of krypton, 200 nCi is 200 nanocurie. Not a lot. You can tinker all you want with it but do not put it under your pillow, swallow it, file, saw or drill it, give it to your childern .... you got the idea ... and dispose of it properly.

replies.author: @Str8Reckless63
replies.text: @hanslepoeter5167 again thank you for sharing and informing me. It really means a lot 🫡

author: @Corialtavi
text: I have seen converters to take cheaper battery packs from one manufacturer for use on a higher end manufacturers drill (or other battery powered tool). I'm sure people would realise you would have to take into consideration the voltage you are drawing from the battery to the tool & the electronics installed to prevent this implemented on some high end stuff but I have been thinking about how to mould myself a nice little converter as it looks quite do-able. I've started picking up some very cheap 12v tool sets now everyone has moved over to 18 & 24v to make myself a set that would use the same battery (these old ones seem to have less anti-tamper electronics to interfere).

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: Why did you stop ? I virtually take apart everything before I throw it away, keeping screws and usefull parts. I learn along the way and I always have a screw, nut or washer when I need one.

replies.author: @martinX74
replies.text: At the moment i'm taking apart my neighbors aio printer before i scrap it. Good stuff inside 🤘

replies.author: @DaymeEdna
replies.text: I did the same, and to this day my curiosity still drives me to pull all manner of things apart, though I’m yet to put any of them back together again.

replies.author: @GMoney-B
replies.text:
Same here. It has become extremely extremely useful. I have been able to fix so many things and not afraid to take anything apart to fix things. 

Most people just think of electronics and devices as nebulous magical things, which they are magical in being amazing, but people are ultimately powerless when it comes to even the thought of even thinking about fixing something. 

It has helped me to understand and learn all the different methods how metal and plastic parts are fastened, attached and clipped together, how things are parts are before I even take something apart to know how to get it apart, and some super super basic knowledge of electronics that helps me a little bit. I would love to learn more though to be able to build and fix more things though.

author: @AndyFletcherX31
text: Those battery packs are designed not to be interchangeable between manufacturers by moulding raised bits in the case next to the connections. If you shave the raised bits off with a sharp knife you can then swap 18V packs between products and chargers at will which can be really helpful when the battery on your drill goes flat but the one on your impact driver is still good. You can see these raised bits if you try to clip a different battery pack onto your tool and find it won't slide into position.

replies.author: @anthonybailey7628
replies.text: I saw a video sometime ago about various adapters you can buy to enable batteries from one manufacturer to be used with another manufacturers cordless tools. I can’t find it now to give a link but it is on you tube somewhere, I think it was posted by an American. It might be worth looking for yourself if you are interested. I know the adapters are available in the states.

replies.author: @GMoney-B
replies.text:
This has opened up my mind. I have a cheap black and decker cordless that works great but the battery has been long gone. Was figuring I could either throw it in the trash which I don’t like being wasteful, or try to learn how to build my own battery pack.

Do you know if all you need is the right voltage? Or do you also need to worry about the amps and or watts?

text: I can't count how many of those I have thrown out over the years. Thanks.

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Rob,
actually I'm in the deep lockdown, so I cannot try myself.
Therefore a quick (silly) question: Does it need any changes to run this powerful motor as a generator?

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text:
If I may answer that : It will work as I generator if it has a permanent magnet as stator. This one probably has. If it has an electromagnet as stator ( series motor ) it might generate due to earth magnetic field to get it going and if a load is present to make sure current flows. You see : when there's no magnetic field in a series motor you can rotate the rotor as much as you like but no voltage will be induced. Once it starts due to earth magnetic field current will flow, stator will be magnetized and it will generate. Sufficient earth magnetic field under correct angle must be present.
So it's an excellent question imho. Not a silly one at all.

replies.author: @scantrain5007
replies.text: @hanslepoeter5167 Many Thanks! Will try when this situation is over ...
